steps.title: Read the structure of the sentence
steps.content: The sentence says Chaucer "didn't write in Modern English; ______ he wrote in what we now call Middle English." The first clause states what is false; the second clause corrects it with what is true. The blank must introduce a word that signals this kind of correction or substitution.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'for this reason,': signals a cause-effect relationship — as if writing in Middle English was caused by not writing in Modern English. There is no causal logic here; the second clause simply replaces the first. ✗
  • Choice B — 'instead,': signals substitution, replacing the negated claim with the actual fact. The pattern "didn't do X; instead, did Y" is a direct match for this sentence's structure. ✓
  • Choice C — 'furthermore,': signals addition of an equally weighted point. But the second clause doesn't add new information — it corrects the false premise in the first clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'likewise,': signals similarity or parallel. The two clauses describe opposites (Modern English vs. Middle English), not parallels. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
